title: Notion
note: My second brain. Everything I know, plan, or want to remember lives here. The closest thing I've found to thinking in software.

title: Todoist
note: Deceptively simple, endlessly reliable. The app that keeps my days from becoming noise.

title: Claude
note: My LLM of choice. The thing that takes an idea in my head and helps make it real faster than I ever thought possible.

title: Apple Podcasts
note: Still the podcast app I reach for after 15 years. Nothing has beaten it for me: simplicity, reliability, and it's always just there.

title: FutMob
note: Everything you need to follow football in one place. Scores, stats, live updates. Built for people who actually care about the game.

title: Nike Run Club
note: The app that got me to 2,700 km and counting.

title: NYT Games
note: A non-negotiable part of my morning. Currently on a Wordle streak with 97% success rate (777 games played to date).

title: AirPods Pro
note: At this point they're just part of my body. The hardware that made everything else on this list better.

title: MacBook Air
note: The machine that does everything and more. Best laptop I've ever owned.

title: Empire of AI
note: The most honest and unsettling account of how AI is actually being built, and who bears the cost. Essential reading if you work in tech.

title: Blood in the Garden
note: The inside story of the New York Knicks' most chaotic era. You don't need to love basketball to love this book. It's really about ambition, dysfunction, and what happens when egos run unchecked.

title: Born to Run
note: Not a rock autobiography. A genuine piece of writing. Springsteen excavates where his drive comes from with a honesty most people never manage in private, let alone in print.

title: Supercommunicators
note: A practical and surprisingly moving book about what actually happens when people connect. Changed how I listen.

title: The Road
note: The bleakest and most beautiful book I've ever read. McCarthy strips language down to nothing and somehow makes it say everything.

title: La Profezia dell'Armadillo
note: Italian graphic novel that hit closer to home than most prose ever has. Funny, melancholic, and painfully recognisable if you grew up in Rome in the 2000s.

title: Hard Fork
note: The best weekly debrief on tech culture. Smart without being smug, and genuinely funny. Rare for anything that covers Silicon Valley.

title: Acquired
note: Long-form deep dives into how great companies were actually built. Every episode is a proper case study. The kind of thing business school should have been.

title: Freakonomics Radio
note: Still the gold standard for making data and economics feel alive. Recent discovery, although it's been going for years.

title: The Diary of a CEO
note: Long, honest conversations with people who've built something real. The interview style gets to things most podcasts don't reach.

title: Easyapple
note: The Italian Apple podcast I've listened to for more than 10 years. Rigorous, opinionated, and always worth the time if you're in the Apple ecosystem.

title: La Riserva
note: Italian football analysis done properly with a fun touch to it. It's like going to a bar with a friend.

title: SmartLess
credit: Jason Bateman, Sean Hayes & Will Arnett
note: Pure joy. Three friends who happen to be funny interviewing people they genuinely love. The antidote to everything heavy on this list.

title: The Interface
credit: Karen Hao, Tom Germain & Nicky Woolf
note: Three sharp journalists debating the tech stories that matter every week. No guests, no jargon. BBC at its best.

title: Born to Run
note: The album that makes you feel like the open road is a real place you can drive to. Side one is as close to perfect as rock gets.

title: Wasting Light
note: Recorded in Dave Grohl's garage on tape. It sounds like it, in the best possible way. Raw, loud, and completely alive.

title: Folklore
note: The album that made a lot of people reassess what they thought about Taylor Swift. Quiet, introspective, and genuinely great songwriting.

title: AM
note: The album that turned Arctic Monkeys from a guitar band into something stranger and more seductive. R U Mine? still sounds like nothing else.

title: Vinyl Record Player
note: The ritual of putting a record on is half the point. Slows everything down in exactly the right way.

title: New Balance 2002R
note: The shoe I keep coming back to. Understated, well-made, and goes with everything. Proof that the best design is the kind you stop noticing.
